data.summary:
Meet our feathered friends, the rock doves! Also known as common or rock pigeons, they're a fascinating species within the Columbidae family. Unlike their domesticated counterparts, wild rock doves have a uniform grey color with black wing bars, found in various open environments across Southern Europe, North Africa, and Western Asia.

They are an abundant species, with over 17 to 28 million birds in Europe alone! The official name for this friendly bird is rock dove, named after their preferred roosting spots - cliffs and rock ledges. These charming creatures are a great example of nature's resilience, as their descendants from escaped domestic pigeons can now be found worldwide.

Rock doves are closely related to the hill pigeon, with 'squabs' being the adorable term for their young ones. Let's cherish and support these remarkable birds in their natural habitats!

Greetings from the world of fascinating avian wonders! Today, let's get acquainted with a unique and captivating bird species – the Speckled Pigeon (Columba guinea), often referred to as the African Rock Pigeon or Guinea Pigeon.

These medium-sized pigeons, about 32–35 cm long, are a common sight in Africa south of the Sahara, particularly around human habitats and cultivation. They are easily recognized by their striking plumage – rufous backs and wings with white spots, blue-grey upperparts and underparts, and red legs. The speckled pigeon's distinct call, a loud "doo-doo-doo," adds to its charm.

Their adaptability is quite remarkable; these birds build large stick nests on rocky outcrops or urban areas, often settling atop covered pergola pillars and on flat roofs under deep eaves. They lay two white eggs and feed mainly on vegetable matter, particularly gathering in large numbers when grain or groundnuts are available.

Although closely related to the Rock Dove, the Speckled Pigeon has a unique lineage that's left its imprint on many domesticated pigeon breeds over time. This friendly and fascinating creature is indeed a treasure in our diverse avian world, so let's cherish and protect them!

Discover the captivating white-collared pigeon, a fascinating avian resident of the Ethiopian Highlands in Eritrea and Ethiopia! This distinctive bird can be found gracefully soaring over countryside surrounding rocky cliffs and gorges or casually perched at town centers. Despite its large size, 32 cm in length, this striking pigeon stands out with its grey-brown body, a slate-grey head, and an eye-catching white hindcollar. And if you're lucky, you might even spot the prominent white wing patches as it takes flight! These birds are not just beautiful but vital parts of their habitat - let's do our part to ensure they continue gracing our skies for generations to come!

In the highlands of central Asia, you'll find the enchanting snow pigeon - a charming bird with a unique charm! These birds are easily recognizable by their distinctive appearance: a blackish head, a white neck collar, and white underparts that gradually turn ashy on the abdomen. Their backs are brownish-grey, adorned with a white patch on the lower back, and they have pale grey wings with three brown bands.

Two subspecies of these delightful creatures can be found - C. l. gradaria in eastern Tibet and adjacent regions, and C. l. leuconota, native to the western Himalayas. Despite their remote habitats, they are common birds, residing in rocky hills across several countries like Afghanistan, Bhutan, China, India, Kazakhstan, Myanmar, Nepal, Pakistan, Tajikistan, and Turkmenistan.

These elusive yet friendly birds can often be spotted foraging in pairs or small groups during the day, feeding on a variety of food sources such as grain, buds, berries, and seeds. As night falls, they return to their roosting spots - cliffs, where they build their untidy but cozy stick nests.

The International Union for Conservation of Nature has assessed the conservation status of these delightful creatures as being of least concern. So, let's do our part in preserving these beautiful snow pigeons and appreciate their beauty in our mountains!

Meet the enchanting hill pigeon (Columba rupestris), also known as the eastern rock dove or Turkestan hill dove. This stout-bodied bird shares similarities with the rock dove but boasts a distinctive white tail-band across its black tail, a paler mantle, and upper wings, and a white patch on its back.

Hailing from diverse locations such as China, Pakistan, India, Nepal, Kazakhstan, North Korea, South Korea, Mongolia, Russia, Tajikistan, Afghanistan, and Turkmenistan, these sociable creatures thrive in rugged, open country between 1,500 to 6,100 meters above sea level. The hill pigeon is closely related to the rock dove but prefers higher altitudes.

A charming aspect of their lifestyle is their tameness—they often frequent human settlements, camps, and pilgrimage routes. Despite facing a decreasing population overall, these birds are abundant and classified as least concern by the IUCN.

As winter turns to summer, hill pigeons migrate to higher altitudes within their range in Pakistan, residing from about 2000 m in winter to up to 5500 m during summer. During their nesting season, these gregarious birds establish dense colonies on cliffs, gorges, and rocky outcrops.

Their feeding habits are flexible, with a primary focus on grains, but they also enjoy green shoots, leaves, and even small mollusks like snails. In some cases, their diet becomes particularly opportunistic, including leftover food, partially digested food from kiang dung, and undigested food from the stomach of kiang carcasses.

If you're fortunate enough to come across a hill pigeon, you'll witness a mesmerizing bowing display similar to that of the rock dove during courtship season. Support these fascinating creatures by learning more about them and encouraging conservation efforts in their habitats.

Meet the Stock Dove, a captivating member of the Columbidae family, fondly known as the doves and pigeons! Originating from the western Palearctic region, this species stretches from Scandinavia to northwest Africa, with some populations migrating to avoid colder climates.

The Stock Dove is a striking beauty, boasting a grey plumage with a blue tint, and adorned with two short wingbars on its tertials and greater coverts, as well as a dark bar at the tip of its tail. Its breast is lightly suffused pinkish-purple, while its neck sports an iridescent patch that shimmers between green and purple.

This charming bird is both social and gregarious, often seen flocking with wood pigeons and occasionally even rock doves. Despite its friendly nature, the Stock Dove remains elusive compared to its more abundant cousin, the common wood pigeon.

In addition to being a delightful sight, the Stock Dove plays a crucial role in maintaining the balance of nature, making it an essential creature worth supporting and cherishing. So, next time you spot one, don't forget to give it a warm welcome!

Nurture your fascination with the common wood pigeon (Columba palumbus), an enchanting large bird native to Europe, western Asia, and North Africa! This feathered friend is part of the Columba family, sharing its habitat with species like the rock dove.

Standing out among its relatives, this captivating creature boasts a larger size, with weights ranging from 300 to 615 grams, and distinctive features such as white markings on the neck and wings, along with a pinkish breast. Its overall appearance is a delightful blend of grey tones and vibrant accents, making it easy to spot in its natural habitat.

As autumn arrives and winter approaches in colder regions, these elegant birds embark on mesmerizing migratory journeys, while those living in warmer climates remain as year-round residents. In many European towns and cities, including Great Britain, you'll find these charismatic creatures gracing parks and gardens, adding a touch of nature to urban landscapes.

By supporting the common wood pigeon and its habitat, we contribute to maintaining a balance in ecosystems, ensuring that future generations can enjoy the beauty and wonder of this remarkable species. Let's cherish these avian friends and spread awareness about their importance!

Meet our feathered friend, the Trocaz pigeon! This unique bird calls the stunning island of Madeira, Portugal its home. With a predominantly grey body and a pinkish breast, it stands out with its silvery neck patch and lack of white wing markings - a trait that sets it apart from its close relative, the common wood pigeon.

What makes this pigeon truly fascinating is its enchanting call - a six-note cooing, softer and deeper than the wood pigeon's, which echoes through the lush laurel forests where they reside. Despite its bulky appearance with its long tail, this bird boasts a swift and direct flight.

Sadly, the Trocaz pigeon faced significant challenges due to human settlement and habitat loss in Madeira. Their population declined sharply, leading them to disappear from Porto Santo Island altogether. However, protection efforts for their forest habitats and hunting bans have helped the species recover, and they are no longer endangered today.

Let's cherish and support these captivating creatures as they continue to grace our world with their presence!

In the vast expanse of Central Asia and Afghanistan, you'll find the captivating Yellow-eyed Pigeon (Columba eversmanni), a remarkable avian companion with a gentle demeanor. With an average size of 30 cm and weighing between 183 to 234 grams, this charming pigeon stands out due to its distinctive appearance. Its upper parts are a subtle blend of grey and brown, while the crown, throat, breast, and wings boast a captivating pinkish-purple sheen.

The bird's most striking feature is its yellow eyes, surrounded by a bare area of skin that adds a touch of vibrancy to its otherwise muted coloring. This pigeon could be mistaken for other species like the rock dove or hill pigeon, but its unique features set it apart. During the breeding season, you might even hear its faint and melodious "oo-oo-oo" call echoing across its territory.

Sadly, this beautiful creature has faced a decline in numbers over the years due to hunting, earning it the "vulnerable" status by the International Union for Conservation of Nature. Despite these challenges, we can all do our part to support and protect this fascinating bird species, ensuring its continued existence for future generations to admire.

Discover the captivating Somali Pigeon, a unique bird species native to Somalia! This fascinating creature belongs to the Columbidae family, sharing similarities with the stock dove and yellow-eyed pigeon.

The Somali Pigeon boasts a mid-sized frame with striking features such as pale grey feathers, a mauve cap, vibrant yellow eyes, and an iridescent collar adorned with bronze-green hues. In flight, its bluish-gray rump, dark wing edges, and tail bands add to its allure.

This enchanting bird can be found predominantly on the ground, often foraging in arid coastal environments, especially around cliffs and hills. Despite its striking beauty, it's not always easy to spot due to its preference for areas with minimal vegetation.

The Somali Pigeon's vocalizations are as intriguing as its appearance. It emits a distinctive "wuk-wuk-wuk-ooooh" sound, along with other pigeon-like coos.

Despite being featured on the one shilling coin of Somaliland, this elusive bird is in need of attention. Its population size and trend remain unclear, and it lacks legal protection. Furthermore, competition from the expanding speckled pigeon may pose a threat. Efforts to study and protect this captivating species are underway, making its conservation an exciting journey for bird enthusiasts!

In the heart of Spain's breathtaking Canary Islands, a captivating bird called Bolle's pigeon resides among the lush laurel forests. Named after Carl Bolle, a German naturalist who first recognized it, this striking avian stands out with its larger size and unique dark grey plumage, contrasting from other island pigeons.

Its distinctive features include a reddish beak and head that glimmers in slate grey or greenish metallic shades for males, with a pinkish breast. The voice of Bolle's pigeon echoes through the forest with a guttural, mournful cooing.

As a frugivore, this enchanting bird delights in feasting on various fruits like Azores laurel, Viñátigo, Til, and faya. A resident breeder, Bolle's pigeon builds elaborate nests high up in trees, laying one or two eggs during the winter months.

You can find this remarkable bird swooping low and fast over laurel forests with its quick, rhythmic flight, characterized by sharp wing flicks. Supporting this charming species ensures the preservation of these beautiful forest ecosystems and a continued symphony of life in the enchanting Canary Islands.

Welcome to the captivating world of the Laurel Pigeon! Native to the enchanting Canary Islands of Spain, this unique bird species has made its home among the lush laurel forests, earning itself the title symbol of the island of La Gomera.

A sight to behold, the Laurel Pigeon stands at about 40 cm tall, sporting a dark brown and grey body reminiscent of a large wood pigeon, with striking red underparts and a pale grey tail adorned with a broad, whitish band. Its bill is pink with a white tip, eyes are vibrant orange, and it boasts an iridescent green gloss on the crown and hindneck that transitions to a pink hue on its upper mantle.

Sadly, this enchanting creature has faced numerous challenges, with habitat loss due to forest clearance being a major cause of population decline. Added threats include hunting, predation by introduced species, and pesky rats. However, efforts are underway to protect laurel forests, ban hunting, and preserve this endangered bird's habitat.

The Laurel Pigeon is not just visually striking but also a fascinating creature in its flight and calls. It soars through the air with a swift, direct flight, occasionally flashing a sharp wing flick characteristic of pigeons. When taking off, it does so with an audible clatter. Listen closely to hear its distinctive call - a hoarse, hiccuped cooing that echoes through the forest.

These birds prefer steep slopes, escarpments, and gullies, inhabiting dry forests, laurel forests, Canary pine forests, and even cultivated areas. Their nests are built in tree bases or under rocks in shady, rocky areas with abundant shrubby vegetation. The breeding season varies between islands but typically extends from January to September, peaking between April and June.

Despite the challenges they face, Laurel Pigeons are prolific breeders, often starting families as young as their first year. Nesting occurs throughout the year, particularly February to June, with intricate displays similar to those of common wood pigeons. Join us in supporting this extraordinary species and helping it flourish in its magical home.

Meet our feathered friend, the enchanting Afep Pigeon! Originating from the lush African tropical rainforest, this delightful bird goes by various names - the African woodpigeon, grey woodpigeon, or simply 'afep' in the Bulu language of Cameroon.

This beautiful creature boasts a unique appearance with its pale grey neck and pinkish-grey body contrasting against dark grey wings, a white throat and belly, and striking red eyes. With a call that echoes a rhythmic "doo doo doo" or "whu whu whu whu-WHU", the Afep Pigeon is surely hard to miss!

These social birds primarily dwell in lowland forests but can be found locally at higher altitudes in places like Cameroon and Uganda. They have a diet that ranges from fruit, berries, grain, seeds, and even termites!

The Afep Pigeon is known for its family-oriented behavior - during the dry season's second half, the female lays a single white egg, which both parents diligently incubate. The chick is fed with 'crop milk' after hatching and begins eating solid food a few days later. After 20 to 25 days, the little one takes its first flight!

Delightfully, this captivating species is classified as of least concern by the IUCN, with a stable population throughout their natural habitat across southern Cameroon, Gabon, and northwestern Angola. You can enjoy the soothing sounds of an Afep Pigeon call on Xeno-canto or witness their beauty through photos, videos, and observations at Cornell Lab of Ornithology's Birds of the World. Support this charming bird by learning more about it and spreading the word!

Meet the captivating African olive pigeon, a majestic resident in the lush canopies of eastern and southern Africa! With their striking appearance, these pigeons are hard to miss - males exhibit maroon wings speckled with white spots, grey heads adorned with yellow patches, and long neck plumage streaked in maroon and white. Females resemble the males but are a bit more subdued.

These fascinating creatures call cool and moist forests their home, often found high above 1400 meters. They're not shy about venturing to mountain fynbos, second growth, clearings, or even agricultural lands when opportunities arise. What sets them apart, however, is their unique dietary habit - the African olive pigeon has been observed eating clay soil to supplement their acidic fruit and berry-based diet with essential minerals and as a pH buffer!

Supporting these beautiful birds can make a difference in preserving this remarkable species. Let's help them continue their intriguing geophagy and maintain the balance of our ecosystems!

Oh, hello there! Let's chat about a charming feathered friend - the Cameroon olive pigeon (Columba sjostedti). This little beauty can be found gracing the landscapes of Cameroon, Bioko, and other parts of the Cameroon line.

Sporting a unique blend of grey, green, and purple feathers, it's quite a sight to behold! But what makes this pigeon truly special is its delightful diet - it loves to nibble on fruits from Polyscias fulva trees and waterberry trees. So, if you ever find yourself in their habitat, keep an eye out for these dapper birds munching away on delicious fruit treats! Supporting them means helping preserve the beauty of their natural homes, and who wouldn't want a piece of that?

Discover the captivating São Tomé olive pigeon, a rare and unique bird species found exclusively on the island of São Tomé off Africa's western coast! Standing about 37 to 40 cm tall, this enchanting creature boasts a striking appearance with a grayish head, a maroon body dotted with small white spots, and vibrant yellow legs and beak.

The olive pigeon calls several parts of São Tomé its home, including Chamiço in the northwest, Lagoa Amelia, Zampalma, Nova Ceilão, Bombaim, and Rio Io Grande near Formoso Pequeno in the central massif, the valleys of Xufexufe and Ana Chaves in the southwest, and west of Água Izé, Pico Maria Fernandes, and north of São João dos Angolares in the southeast.

This charming bird shares its home with three other endangered species: the São Tomé ibis, São Tomé oriole, and São Tomé scops owl. Together, they form a symphony of life that relies heavily on the preservation of their shared lowland rainforest habitat for continued survival. So, let's cherish these precious feathered friends and protect their home for future generations to admire!

Welcome to the captivating world of the Speckled Wood Pigeon! Also known as Hodgson's pigeon or the jungle pigeon, this medium-sized bird is a fascinating creature that graces the hills and forests of Asia.

With males boasting a distinctive pinkish-silver breast speckled with black spots and maroon belly, while females show off a more subtle coloration, these birds are truly a sight to behold. They can be found in various countries such as India, China, Nepal, Bhutan, and Afghanistan, living in evergreen and semi-evergreen hill forests at elevations of 1800 to 4000 meters.

What makes these birds even more intriguing is their diet, which mainly consists of acorns, berries, and fruits, making them a vital part of their ecosystem. They breed from May to August, nesting high above ground, and in winter, they descend based on food availability.

The Speckled Wood Pigeon has an interesting history, having been formally described by the co-founder of the Zoological Society of London, Nicholas Aylward Vigors, in 1832. Another person associated with this species is naturalist Brian Houghton Hodgson, who sent a description prior to 1832 but was not published at the time.

So, let's do our part and support these beautiful creatures by learning more about them and spreading awareness. After all, every small action counts in preserving the wonders of nature!

Meet the striking White-naped Pigeon, a captivating bird species that calls the Western High Plateau and other enchanting locations across Africa home! These birds are easily recognized by their elegant white necks adorning black bodies. Living in forests, these little friends can also be found thriving in coffee plantations – a testament to their adaptability.

However, they face a challenge that requires our help: habitat destruction. Many of the forests where they reside are being cleared away. Fortunately, you can make a difference by supporting organizations working towards forest preservation! Let's rally together to protect these delightful birds and preserve the beauty of Africa for generations to come.

Discover the magnificent Comoro olive pigeon, a unique resident of the enchanting Comoro Islands! This extraordinary bird stands out among others due to its impressive size, boasting a dark plumage that adds a touch of mystery. The young ones showcase a striking yellow beak, while their older counterparts sport a more subdued green or brown bill.

Listen carefully and you'll hear the captivating, deep coo that echoes through the forests - "guk-ohoooo hoo hooo". It is found primarily in Mont Karthala, Comoros, and Mayotte, with a higher concentration in the latter island.

Sadly, this fascinating bird faces challenges due to habitat loss and hunting on each of its inhabited islands. With a small population that may be fewer than 10,000 individuals, the International Union for Conservation of Nature has classified it as 'near threatened'. Let's join together in supporting and protecting these beautiful birds to ensure they continue to captivate future generations with their charm.

In the enchanting world of Southeast Asia, a charming bird named the Ashy Wood Pigeon (Columba pulchricollis) resides in lush temperate forests. With its exquisite appearance and fascinating habits, this avian marvel is truly captivating!

Spotting the Ashy Wood Pigeon can be an exciting adventure across various countries such as Nepal, Bhutan, India, Tibet, China, Laos, Myanmar, Thailand, and even Taiwan. Although we don't know exactly how many of these lovely creatures there are worldwide, the population in Taiwan ranges from 10,000 to an astounding 100,000 breeding pairs!

What makes this pigeon especially interesting is its unique reproductive habits. A single white egg is typically found in a clutch, though double eggs have been discovered. The incubation period for these precious eggs lasts around 21 to 23 days, and the little ones take approximately 28 days to spread their wings and venture out into the world!

So next time you're exploring the beautiful forests of Southeast Asia, keep an eye out for this lovely bird. Its elegant beauty and rare sightings make it a truly enchanting experience worth cherishing!

Discover the captivating Sri Lankan Wood Pigeon, a native bird species residing exclusively in the lofty mountains of Sri Lanka! This remarkable creature can often be spotted nesting within damp evergreen woodlands.

Standing at 36 cm tall, this pigeon sports a fascinating appearance – its upperparts and tail are adorned with dark grey feathers, while its head, underparts, and belly are lilac. A distinctive black-and-white chessboard pattern graces its nape, adding to its charm.

This enchanting bird is not only visually appealing but also vocal – during the breeding season, it emits an owl-like 'hoo' call that echoes through the woods. Its diet primarily consists of vegetation, and you might be surprised to learn that it has a quick flight pattern characterized by regular beats and sharp wing flicks, common among pigeons.

If you're ever in the Horton Plains National Park, don't forget to keep an eye out for this stunning wood-pigeon! This fascinating species is not just a sight to behold but also plays a vital role in maintaining the balance of the ecosystem within its Sri Lankan habitat.

Meet our feathered friends, the Nilgiri wood pigeons! These magnificent birds are native to the lush forests of southwestern India's Western Ghats, where they reside in hilltops and valleys alike. Standing out with their large size and dark grey bodies, they are easily spotted by the distinctive checkerboard pattern on their necks.

The Nilgiri wood pigeons are not only visually striking but also play a crucial role as nature's gardener. These friendly birds feast on various fruits, particularly those from the family Lauraceae, aiding in the dispersal of seeds for many forest trees. They even show some flexibility by occasionally snacking on small insects too!

Their unique calls are a melodious series of hoots and coos that echo through the forests, making them an enchanting addition to their woodland homes. As they move throughout the forests in search of fruit, these charming creatures add a touch of elegance and wonder to their environment. Let's cherish and protect these fascinating birds for generations to come!

Nestled within the lush, tropical landscapes of Indonesia and Malaysia, the enchanting Silvery Pigeon (Columba argentina), also known as the silvery wood-pigeon or grey wood-pigeon, is a truly captivating species.

This remarkable bird, with its distinctive plumage that shimmers in pale silvery grey and black feathers, is not only visually striking but also shares an extraordinary trait with another pigeon species – the Pied Imperial Pigeon. Their unique color pattern, unusual for a Columba pigeon, could be seen as a testament to nature's intricate design, possibly even a case of mimicry that offers them a better chance against predators.

The Silvery Pigeon is a slightly smaller cousin to the Pied Imperial Pigeon, with distinctive features like a conspicuous dark red or purplish eye-wattle, sloping forehead, and bluish-grey feet mottled with varying amounts of red. A closer look at this endearing bird reveals these unique characteristics that set it apart from others in its family.

Despite being believed extinct for many years, recent discoveries on Masokut Island and Simeulue have rekindled hope and excitement about the presence of this extraordinary species. Let's do our part to ensure these mesmerizing creatures continue to grace the skies of their enchanting homeland.

Greetings! Let's chat about the captivating Pale-capped Pigeon (Columba punicea), also known as the Purple Wood Pigeon. This large, enchanting bird with a length of 36–40.5 cm can be found scattered across parts of India, Southeast Asia, and even Sri Lanka!

The Pale-capped Pigeon boasts a unique appearance; it's predominantly chestnut brown, with a striking contrast - a pale crown on the male, or a more brownish grey crown on the female. Their feathers shimmer with hints of green or amethyst, making them quite a sight to behold!

You might encounter these charming birds in various habitats, from primary evergreen forests to open deciduous dipterocarp forests and even agricultural fields. During non-breeding seasons, they may also be spotted in mangroves and coastal habitats.

What makes the Pale-capped Pigeon truly special is their diet. They're frugivores, feeding on a variety of fruit, and are known to favor Cinnamon trees. They even forage in small groups within the tree canopies, creating a mesmerizing spectacle!

Despite their enchanting presence, these pigeons are relatively uncommon in most areas. So, if you happen to spot one, consider yourself lucky and remember to cherish this encounter. Supporting them means preserving not just a beautiful bird, but also the diverse habitats they call home!

Greetings! Let's embark on a brief journey to the exotic lands of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, where you'll meet the captivating Andaman wood pigeon (Columba palumboides). With its striking appearance, this beautiful bird is a close relative of the green imperial pigeon and rock dove.

The Andaman wood pigeon stands out with its distinctive features – a white head crowned by a red beak tipped in yellow, and a sleek black body. This rare beauty is found predominantly on North Passage Island, making it quite the treasure to spot! As 'near threatened' species, these delightful creatures need our support. They are estimated to number between 2,500 and 10,000 mature individuals. By learning about them and taking steps to protect their habitat, we can help ensure that this amazing bird continues to grace the skies of the Andaman Islands for generations to come!

Discover the captivating Bonin wood pigeon, a unique species once thriving in the verdant forests of the Ogasawara Islands, south of Japan! This mesmerizing bird was adorned with iridescent shades of greyish-black, violet, amethyst, turquoise, and golden green.

Sadly, this beautiful creature is now extinct due to habitat loss, hunting, and predation by introduced species such as rats and cats. Their last known specimen was found in Nakodo-jima, Japan, in 1889. Despite its tragic fate, the Bonin wood pigeon's eggs were laid with a purpose - typically two at a time - and would hatch within 17 to 19 days, mirroring the development of their extant wood pigeon relatives.

Let us cherish the memory of this extraordinary bird and do our best to protect other precious species from meeting a similar fate!

Meet the White-headed Pigeon, a captivating bird native to the east coast of Australia! This species stands out with its striking white head, neck, breast, and belly, accompanied by slate grey wings, tail, and upperparts that shimmer with green or purple iridescence.

The White-headed Pigeon can be found from Cooktown, Queensland to southern New South Wales, even expanding into eastern Victoria. While their numbers have decreased since the colonization of Australia, they have thrived in urban areas due to the presence of introduced Camphor laurels.

These elusive birds are often solitary or found in small flocks, but their loud, gruff call can be heard echoing through the trees: "WHOO!" followed by a deep "uk" sound. Their primary diet consists of fruits and seeds, particularly the invasive camphor laurel, making them an essential part of Australia's ecosystem.

With a wide range and growing population, the White-headed Pigeon is considered a least-concern species. Supporting these beautiful birds helps maintain balance in their environment and ensures that their distinctive call continues to resonate through Australian forests for years to come!

The charming Ryukyu wood pigeon, also known as the silver-banded or silver-crescented pigeon, was a unique species of bird that once thrived in the lush Laurel forests across various islands in Okinawa, Japan. With its distinct appearance featuring a large white patch on its lower hind neck and bronzy-green scapulars and back, this pigeon shared similarities with other Japanese wood pigeons but had a touch of silver sparkle that set it apart.

Sadly, due to habitat destruction caused by human activities like settlement and agriculture, the Ryukyu wood pigeon is now an extinct species. The last sightings were recorded in 1904 on Okinawa, and on other islands by 1936, as a result of deforestation prior to World War II.

Despite its tragic fate, it's essential that we remember this beautiful creature and appreciate its unique beauty. If ever possible, efforts can be made to preserve its former habitats and protect the remaining forested areas in Okinawa. Let's take a moment to honor the Ryukyu wood pigeon and hope for the day when we might see a bird like it soaring through the skies once more.

Discover the captivating metallic pigeon (Columba vitiensis), a medium-sized bird that graces various islands across the Pacific! With its iridescent purple and green crown, black wings, and distinctive purplish feet, it's easy to spot this striking avian in its natural habitat.

Each of the eight living subspecies boasts unique characteristics, making them a delight for bird enthusiasts. For instance, the Samoan Metallic Pigeon (C. v. castaneiceps) stands out due to its purple-chestnut crown and consistently grey ventral plumage. On the other hand, the New Caledonia Metallic Pigeon (C. v. hypoenochroa) is known for its vibrant amethyst-glossed underparts in males.

While some subspecies are endemic to specific islands like Samoa and Lord Howe Island, others can be found across a wider range of locations, such as the Lesser Sunda Islands and the Philippines. Regrettably, a few subspecies have already become extinct.

Supporting these fascinating birds is crucial for their continued survival in the wild. By learning about them and spreading awareness, we can help ensure that the metallic pigeon's enchanting beauty continues to shine across our oceans!

In the vast landscapes of East Asia, you'll find a remarkable creature known as the Black Wood Pigeon or Japanese Wood Pigeon (Columba janthina). This is the largest pigeon species in the region, boasting an impressive size between 37 cm to 43.5 cm long, and a weight of about 550 grams!

The Black Wood Pigeon sports a captivating, dark appearance with iridescent shades of green or purple on its crown, shoulders, and neck sides. Its beak is a striking blend of greenish blue and ivory to pale yellow, while its legs are a vibrant red. The species is home to several subspecies, each with subtle differences in plumage.

Unlike many other bird species, the Black Wood Pigeon does not exhibit sexual dimorphism—both males and females look alike, although juveniles tend to have paler plumage compared to adults. In flight, it resembles a crow, with large wings and a slightly fanned tail.

Sadly, this striking pigeon faces challenges in its habitat, including habitat loss, degradation, deforestation, and hunting. It resides primarily on small islands in the East China Sea, such as the Ryukyu Islands, Iwo Jima, Bonin Islands, and along the southern coasts of Korea and Japan.

These fascinating birds enrich their environment and deserve our support. By preserving their habitats, we can ensure that future generations will have the opportunity to marvel at this remarkable creature!

Discover the captivating lemon dove, a fascinating pigeon species native to the lush montane forests of sub-Saharan Africa! This charming bird is easily recognized by its unique combination of brownish-grey plumage, a cinnamon brown breast, and distinctive white markings on its head.

What sets the lemon dove apart from other pigeons is its strong affinity for terrestrial life, making it a true ground-dweller among its avian relatives. With seven recognized subspecies found across Africa, from Sierra Leone to South Africa, these birds can be found at altitudes ranging from 100 to 3,000 meters.

Fond of various small fruits, seeds, mollusks, and insects, the lemon dove is a valuable member of its ecosystem. Not only are they common, but the International Union for Conservation of Nature has rated their conservation status as "least concern." So, lend your support to these enchanting creatures and enjoy the beauty of nature with every sighting of the lemon dove!

Welcome to the world of the enchanting Eastern Bronze-naped Pigeon! This captivating bird is a native resident, gracing countries like Angola, Kenya, and South Africa with its charming presence. Its name is a tribute to Adulphe Delegorgue, a collector who shared the joy of discovering it.

With sleek, bronze-hued feathers adorning its nape (the back of its neck), this pigeon stands out in the heart of Africa's diverse wildlife. By supporting these birds, you become part of a cherished circle, helping to preserve the vibrant tapestry of life across our beautiful continent.

Discover the captivating western bronze-naped pigeon (Columba iriditorques)! This medium-sized bird, part of the Turturoena subgenus, can be found throughout Africa's tropical rainforests. The western bronze-naped pigeon boasts a striking appearance, with an adult male weighing around 130 grams and sporting a dark bluish grey head, contrasted by a dark vinous belly and breast. Adult females, on the other hand, have cinnamon-reddish brown crowns and greyish chestnut underparts.

One of the most intriguing features of this bird is its tail – upon landing, a pale, broad terminal tail band becomes apparent, adding a unique touch to its charm. Despite being widespread across Africa, it's comforting to know that the IUCN Red List rates the species as least concern due to its stable population and vast range. This vibrant bird is truly a fascinating addition to Africa's diverse wildlife!

Meet the Island Bronze-Naped Pigeon, a captivating bird species native to the Gulf of Guinea islands! Standing at just 28 cm tall, this small yet striking pigeon boasts an array of colors that will leave you in awe. From its slate-grey head and grey wings, to its buffy-white throat and ashy-grey breast, the pigeon's appearance is nothing short of fascinating!

But what truly sets this species apart is its unique pink or green neck and back, along with its rufous neck feathers. The Island Bronze-Naped Pigeon can be found living in rainforests, secondary forests, and plantations on São Tomé, Príncipe, and Annobón islands.

Known for its love of fruits and berries, the pigeon is a vital part of its ecosystem as a frugivore. The species breeds from November to January on Príncipe and February onwards on São Tomé, building sturdy nests in trees and laying one or two eggs.

Sadly, the Island Bronze-Naped Pigeon is facing challenges due to hunting, causing a decline in its population. But with our support, we can help protect this beautiful creature and ensure it remains a part of these islands' rich biodiversity for years to come!

Meet the Yellow-legged Pigeon, a charming bird species in the Columbidae family! This unique creature can be found gracefully roaming the Bismarck and Solomon archipelagos. With its distinct yellow legs, slender body, and chestnut-brown plumage, it's quite an eye-catching sight!

Despite its captivating appearance, the Yellow-legged Pigeon faces challenges due to habitat loss. However, recent research has provided some good news – this species is not as rare as previously thought, which led to a downgrade in its conservation status from Endangered to Vulnerable in 2008.

Let's do our part to protect these wonderful creatures and ensure they continue to delight us with their presence for years to come!

Discover the fascinating passenger pigeon, a remarkable bird once abundant across North America! Named after its migratory habits, this species (Ectopistes migratorius) was not only found in eastern forests but also flocked together in enormous numbers, making it the most common bird in the region with an estimate of over 3 billion individuals.

Males stood out with their gray upperparts, lighter underparts, and iridescent bronze feathers on the neck, contrasting the duller, browner females. Juveniles resembled females but lacked the male's striking iridescence. These social birds were known for their communal roosting and breeding behaviors, believed to help in finding food and predator protection.

Unfortunately, due to excessive hunting, habitat loss, and population decline, the passenger pigeon became extinct by the early 20th century. The last confirmed wild bird was shot in 1902, and Martha, thought to be the last captive passenger pigeon, passed away in 1914 at the Cincinnati Zoo. This heartbreaking tale serves as a sobering reminder of anthropogenic extinction and the importance of conserving our natural world.

Meet the White-Crowned Pigeon, a charming bird native to the Caribbean islands! This captivating creature is not just found in popular vacation destinations like Cuba, Jamaica, and the Bahamas, but also graces the southern coasts of Central America.

John James Audubon himself painted these delightful birds, immortalizing their beauty in his famous work, Birds of America. With a length of 29 to 35 cm and a weight ranging from 150 to 301 grams, they are similar in size to the common rock pigeon but have a more elongated tail and sleeker body.

Their plumage is a striking mix of shades, ranging from slate gray to nearly black. The most captivating feature, however, is their iridescent nape patch, which glimmers under good light in a mesmerizing barred white and green pattern. Their distinctive crown patch can vary in shade from bright white in males to more gray-white in females.

Don't miss the chance to listen to their unique call – a deep, resonant coo-cura-coo or coo-croo that adds to the tropical charm of their habitat. Despite their captivating nature, these birds are often overlooked, so let's show them some love and appreciate their beauty!

Discover the majestic Scaled Pigeon (Patagioenas speciosa)! This large tropical dove is a common resident across semi-open forests from Mexico to South America and Trinidad. Known for its striking appearance, adult males boast a mainly purple-brown plumage with a scaly neck and underparts, while females are a more subdued brown.

The Scaled Pigeon is not only fascinating in looks but also in flight – swift, high, and direct, with the occasional sharp wing flick that's characteristic of its kind. Unfortunately, it's often hunted due to its wariness.

These birds are named after their distinctive sound – a deep cooing cro ku-ks, different from other relatives like the white-crowned pigeon. Solitary in nature, they feed mainly on forest fruits and seeds but have been observed feeding on green leaves and sometimes crops as well.

Despite being hunted, these beautiful birds are worth supporting due to their unique characteristics and contributions to their ecosystems. It's fascinating to note that Scaled Pigeons have even been spotted nesting on ferns at ground level and in the canopy of tree ferns!

Discover the fascinating Picazuro Pigeon, a South American native and a common sight across Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay! This large bird boasts a wingspan of up to 22 inches and can weigh as much as a pound. Sporting a brown body with white dashes on its neck and darker wing patterns, it shares a resemblance with the spotted dove.

Not only does this adaptable species call various habitats—from dense forests to agricultural lands—home, but it also thrives all year round! Its survival skills are remarkable, as it feeds mainly on seeds and grain found on the ground.

In addition, the Picazuro Pigeon demonstrates unwavering devotion to its young. Nests are built from sticks and house a single egg laid per season. Both parents incubate the egg and care for the hatched chick by feeding it 'crop milk' before switching to regurgitated seeds.

This resilient species, with an estimated global extent of occurrence of 5,800,000 km2, deserves our support in preserving its natural habitats and continuing its population growth. Let's lend a helping hand to ensure this remarkable bird remains a cherished part of South America for generations to come!

Discover the charming Scaly-naped Pigeon, a captivating bird species native to the Caribbean! With its large stature of 14-16 inches, this avian friend stands out in the crowd. What sets it apart is the unique texture of its nape, resembling a scale-like pattern on its maroon neck, earning it both common and scientific names.

The Scaly-naped Pigeon can be found across the Greater Antilles (except for Jamaica and the Bahamas) and the Lesser Antilles. These birds thrive in various habitats, including rainforests and lowland woodlands, often near human settlements. However, their population is under threat due to hunting and hurricanes that impact their natural surroundings.

These gentle creatures are usually seen alone or in small groups, feeding on fruits and seeds from trees. They share their habitat with Rock doves, Eared doves, and Zenaida doves. Despite their wary nature, they're worth supporting for the joy and beauty they bring to our world!

Meet the majestic Band-tailed Pigeons! Hailing from the vast lands of the Americas, these are among the largest pigeons you'll find in this region. With an average length of 33 to 40 cm and weights ranging between 226 – 268 grams, they possess thick-based, pointed wings that span up to 66 cm.

Their feathers are predominantly gray, with a faint pinkish hue on their heads and underparts, especially in adult males. The belly is almost white, and the tail has a pale lower half contrasted by a darker band at the base – hence the English name. Their bills and feet are yellow, making them easily recognizable at close range.

These pigeons share closest ties with two other species: the Chilean Pigeon and the Ring-tailed Pigeon. Six subspecies exist, divided into two groups that differ primarily in bill color. The northern group sports a black-tipped yellow bill, while the southern group's bill is entirely yellow.

The Band-tailed Pigeon shares a close evolutionary relationship with the extinct Passenger Pigeon. This connection has sparked interest in exploring their potential role in efforts to revive the passenger pigeon species. Let's cherish and support these fascinating creatures!

Meet the captivating Chilean Pigeon, a mesmerizing bird native to Chile and Argentina! This charming species stands out with its vibrant plumage - adult males are adorned in rich reddish purple, while females and juveniles showcase softer shades of gray. The Chilean Pigeon's distinctive features include an iridescent green nape, a narrow white stripe across the top, and a striking black band on its tail.

These beautiful birds are often found in the lush forests of Araucaria and Nothofagus, but they are also known to venture out into open habitats near their forest homes. As fruitarian birds, they spend much of their time foraging high up in trees, yet they won't shy away from seeds in open areas when the opportunity arises.

During the breeding season, which spans from December to May, Chilean Pigeons nest in colonies, with several hundred pairs taking residence deep within the forest's interior, often in a bamboo thicket. Their nests are simple platforms of sticks that reveal their single egg. Listen carefully, and you might hear their hauntingly beautiful song - a series of deep hoos or a melodic double hooo-HOOOO hooo-HOOOO hooo-HOOOO...

Although the Chilean Pigeon is currently classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, deforestation of old-growth forests poses a significant threat. By supporting these magnificent birds and their habitats, we can help ensure that their enchanting songs continue to echo through the trees for generations to come.

Meet the enchanting Ring-tailed Pigeon! This vibrant bird can only be found in the beautiful landscapes of Jamaica, primarily residing within the John Crow Mountains, the eastern Blue Mountains, and the Cockpit Country.

Distinguished by its unique appearance, this pigeon boasts a variety of colors on its adult male: reddish to pinkish underparts, a metallic green or bronze patch on its hindneck, brownish gray upperparts, and a slate band across its middle tail. The female version shares similar traits but with an olive or brown cast to the wings, redder underparts, and a less metallic hindneck.

What makes these pigeons truly fascinating is their diet – they exclusively feed on fruit, often hanging upside-down while foraging in trees! Although currently classified as Vulnerable due to threats like hunting and forest destruction, let's do our part by supporting them and cherishing their existence in this world.

Meet our charming friend, the Bare-eyed Pigeon! This captivating bird species belongs to the Columbidae family and can be found in countries like Colombia, Venezuela, and even the Netherlands Antilles. Interestingly, it's part of a group that some believe shares similar plumage with other pigeons such as the Picazuro, Spot-winged, and Scaled Pigeons.

Standing tall at 32 to 37 cm for males and 30 to 34.5 cm for females, these birds weigh around 274 grams. The Bare-eyed Pigeon's unique feature is the mauve-pink head, neck, and breast contrasted by an orange-brown eye surrounded by bare skin, a blue ring, and reddish-brown outer area.

These birds thrive in arid landscapes of thorn scrub, cactus, and acacia, but you may also find them in mangroves or cultivated areas. Despite being found as high as 400 meters above sea level on the mainland, they are generally much lower near coasts.

What's intriguing is that this species was introduced to Saint Martin island in the Caribbean at the end of 2012, and since then, its population has grown significantly and spread across the entire island!

In terms of diet, the Bare-eyed Pigeon feeds on seeds and fruits from various trees, vines, and cultivated crops. They primarily forage in trees and shrubs but also venture onto the ground when needed.

Breeding season seems to be triggered by rain, and their nests are delicate platforms of twigs found high up in trees or shrubs. These nests are so thin that you can see the single egg through them from below!

The Bare-eyed Pigeon's melodious song is a rhythmic series of coos, creating a soothing symphony in its habitat. Unfortunately, due to heavy hunting, these birds are shy and timid but remain relatively secure according to the IUCN, being classified as Least Concern.

So let's cherish and support this beautiful and fascinating species!

Discover the captivating spot-winged pigeon (Patagioenas maculosa)! Native to Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Paraguay, Peru, and Uruguay, this fascinating bird boasts two distinct subspecies - P. m. maculosa and P. m. albipennis.

The nominate P. m. maculosa sports a vibrant combination of purplish pink, gray, and creamy white, while the adult male of P. m. albipennis showcases a stunning blue-gray plumage with a hint of purple. These unique pigeons live in open woodlands and scrublands, preferring arid to semi-arid regions up to 1000 meters in elevation for P. m. maculosa, and between 2000 and 4300 meters for the blue-gray P. m. albipennis.

These social birds can often be found feeding together on seeds, fruits, and leaves. The diet of P. m. albipenniss is not yet fully understood, but their close relative P. m. maculosa has been observed enjoying a variety of seeds, including rice and sunflower.

Both subspecies breed year-round in Argentina and build fragile nests high up in trees. The spot-winged pigeon's rhythmic song adds a melodious touch to their habitats, making them an essential part of the region's natural symphony. Supporting these beautiful creatures helps maintain the balance in their ecosystems and enriches our world with their striking beauty!

Greetings! Let's take a moment to appreciate the charming Pale-vented Pigeon (Patagioenas cayennensis). Hailing from the warm lands of the Americas, this large pigeon stands out among its family with its unique appearance and distinctive call.

Unlike many pigeons, the males of this species sport a dull purple head, breast, and upperparts adorned with a coppery gloss on the nape. Their underparts are pale grey, with black bills, red legs, iris, and eyering. Females resemble males but are subtler in color, while immatures are a more muted grey-brown.

What sets this pigeon apart is its preferred habitat - they can often be seen soaring high above open areas with trees, such as forest edges or riverbanks. While their flight may remind you of other pigeons, their calls and appearance from close up reveal subtle differences from the scaled pigeon, their closest relative in terms of habits.

The Pale-vented Pigeon is a common sight at feeding areas, particularly when small fruits, berries, or seeds are available. They build nests in small trees and typically lay one white egg. This widespread and common species is classified as of least concern by the IUCN, making it an excellent candidate for support and observation. So, next time you spot one, take a moment to admire this enchanting bird!

Meet the Red-billed Pigeon! This captivating bird is a sight to behold, particularly in its native habitat stretching from southern Texas, USA, to Costa Rica, often spotted in coastal and lowland regions of Mexico and Central America. With a robust build, it's easy to spot this species due to its dark slate-gray body contrasting with a maroon head, neck, and wing coverts. Its eyes are a striking pale red, surrounded by an orange orbital ring, and its bill is red with a yellow tip – but don't expect the iridescent collar plumage commonly found in other pigeons.

What truly sets the Red-billed Pigeon apart is its unique call – a long, high-pitched sound made up of a coooo followed by three cuk-c'-c'-coo notes that ascend in pitch. The noise it creates when taking flight with its wings clapping adds to the bird's charm.

Their distinctive appearance and melodious call make these birds an essential part of the dry forests where they reside, primarily along riversides in Mexico and Central America. So, next time you find yourself exploring this region, keep your eyes peeled for our friendly Red-billed Pigeons!

Meet the humble yet enchanting Plain Pigeon! This fascinating bird is native to the stunning landscapes of the Greater Antilles, gracing Cuba, Hispaniola, Jamaica, and Puerto Rico with its presence. Despite its common appearance, it shares characteristics with larger relatives like the band-tailed pigeon, boasting a striking red-wine wash on its head, neck, breast, and wings.

In flight, the Plain Pigeon's conspicuous wing bar can be spotted, while at rest, it exhibits an overall pale blue-gray hue that might remind you of a distant relative - the common city pigeon! Females are slightly smaller with a more subtle appearance than their male counterparts.

This remarkable species once came close to extinction in Puerto Rico during the 1970s, but thankfully, conservation efforts were introduced and now the Plain Pigeon's population has rebounded to a few thousand individuals. Let's cherish these resilient feathered friends and support their continued survival!

Discover the captivating Maranon Pigeon, a vibrant bird species native to South America! This fascinating friend is known by various names – Maranon pigeon, Peruvian pigeon, or Salvin's pigeon (Patagioenas oenops).

Standing around 34 cm tall for males and 31-34 cm for females, weighing approximately 276 grams, the Maranon Pigeon is a striking bird. Males boast a head, neck, and breast of dull reddish or purple hues, while their upperparts are a beautiful blend of blue-gray to dark gray with hints of chestnut on the mid-back and wings. Females and juveniles have a more subdued appearance, appearing browner than their male counterparts.

This enchanting bird can be found in the lush forests of extreme southeastern Ecuador and southeast into Peru, specifically in the Department of La Libertad. The Maranon Pigeon favors riparian forest but often moves to dryer upslope forest during certain seasons. It thrives at elevations ranging from 850 to 2400 meters above sea level.

These social pigeons can be spotted foraging in small groups and have been observed munching on ripe coca seeds and the fruits of Cordia lutea trees. While little is known about their breeding patterns, their melodious song – a rhythmic series of coos – is sure to delight any who are fortunate enough to hear it.

Sadly, habitat loss and degradation have led to a decline in this species' range and population, earning the Maranon Pigeon a near-threatened status from the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN). Let us work together to protect these enchanting birds and ensure their continued presence in our vibrant world.

Friendly Greetings! Let's chat about the fascinating Plumbeous Pigeon – a charming bird that graces the skies of South and Central America.

This beautiful creature can be found in various countries like Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, French Guiana, Guyana, Panama, Paraguay, Peru, Suriname, and Venezuela. The Plumbeous Pigeon stands tall at 34 cm and weighs between 172 to 231 g.

Male Plumbeous Pigeons sport a dark gray head, neck, and underparts with a pinkish or purplish tinge. Their shoulders, back, rump, wings, and tail are a drab olive to grayish brown. Females share similar features but have less intense purple wash and more prominent neck spots.

What sets this species apart is their distinctive vocalizations, which differ among and sometimes even within subspecies. These differences hint that there may be more than one species involved, adding to their intrigue.

The Plumbeous Pigeon can be found in various parts of its range, from the tropical rainforests of South America to the cloud forests at higher elevations. They are fascinating creatures worth supporting and appreciating for their unique beauty and mysterious calls!

Meet the short-billed pigeon, a fascinating avian companion residing in forests and woodlands of Mexico to Colombia! This captivating bird is part of the Patagioenas family, known for their unique, melodious calls.

At approximately 26.5 cm long and 150 g in weight, these pigeons are relatively large with a wine-purple body, blackish tail and primary feathers, black bill, and purple-red legs and eyes. The female has a slightly muted coloration, while the young bird sports a greyish brown head and upperparts with cinnamon scaling.

The short-billed pigeon's distinctive call is a complex and high-pitched one - coo... cu-COO k’CO coohoo, usually uttered singly or with pauses between calls. You can often spot these friendly duos in tree tops foraging for their favorite foods like mistletoe, fruits, berries, or even seeking out small invertebrates along tracks and roadsides!

In the higher altitudes of southern Central America, you'll find its close relative, the ruddy pigeon. While they share similarities, the short-billed pigeon's call is notably slower and more intricate. So why not lend a supportive hand to these charming creatures by learning their melodious calls and supporting their habitats?

Discover the captivating ruddy pigeon, a fascinating feathered friend that resides in the vibrant regions of Costa Rica and extends southwards to western Ecuador, Bolivia, and central Brazil. This charming bird is part of the Oenoenas clade within the Patagioenas genus, known for their distinct calls and unique appearances.

Standing at approximately 11 inches tall and weighing around 170 grams, the ruddy pigeon showcases a captivating wine-purple color that becomes more rusty on its back. Its tail and primary flight feathers are dark brown, while its bill is sleek black, and its legs are a striking purple-red. The most distinctive feature of this bird's eyes is their ability to change from red to dull yellow in certain populations—a trait not found in other sympatric subspecies.

One of the most enchanting aspects of this pigeon is its call, which is loud and high-pitched, featuring long pauses between each coo. Some populations even sing four-noted songs! In Central America, it can be found soaring in the treetops of highland forests and semi-open woodlands, while in South America, it thrives in the canopies of humid forests from sea level to 1500 meters above sea level.

The ruddy pigeon is a cherished species due to its melodious call and charming appearance. Despite facing potential deforestation, it has been classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. These delightful birds are typically seen in pairs, feasting on fruits, berries—especially mistletoe fruit—and seeking grit on tracks and roadsides. They build simple nests of twigs 5 meters high in small trees, laying a single white egg as they begin their journey to parenthood.

Discover the enchanting Rodrigues pigeon, a fascinating bird once thriving on the Mascarene island of Rodrigues! This charming creature, known from ancient bones and traveler accounts, was similar in size to a tambourine dove and sported a slate grey coat.

Leguat, an early explorer, fondly remembered these gentle birds gathering around their table for melon seeds. Unfortunately, the Rodrigues pigeon is now extinct, likely due to rats invading its nesting sites in the mid-18th century. Remarkably, it may have been a close relative of the dodo, further emphasizing the intriguing connections between these extinct Mascarene island birds.

Discover the fascinating Malagasy turtle dove, a captivating pigeon species that calls several exotic islands home! With its distinct appearance featuring a striking pattern of black and white feathers, this bird is found across Madagascar, Mauritius, Comoros, Réunion, Mayotte, Seychelles, and the Chagos archipelago.

Despite some island populations being somewhat rare, the Malagasy turtle dove as a whole is classified as a species of least concern by experts. Its closest living relative is the elusive pink pigeon, making it an intriguing member of its family. So, let's give these beautiful creatures the support they deserve and celebrate their vibrant existence!

Meet the captivating Dusky Pigeon! This stunning bird is native to the lush rainforests of Colombia and Ecuador, specifically in the Chocó Biogeographic Region. Standing at approximately 24 cm tall, it's easy to spot with its unique coloration - a blend of gray, purple, olive brown, and bronzy gray feathers adorning its head, breast, back, wings, and tail.

Its vibrant eye can be any shade of red, adding an extra touch of charm to this enigmatic bird. The Dusky Pigeon's melodious song is a series of three high-pitched coos, "whoah..pup..pup," while its call is a soothing purr, "rrrow."

Despite the limited knowledge about its foraging behavior and diet, this charming species has been fortunate enough to be classified as Least Concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN). However, deforestation poses a potential threat to the Dusky Pigeon's habitat, making it crucial that we cherish and protect these beautiful creatures.

Discover the European Turtle Dove, a captivating bird species belonging to the Columbidae family. Native to the southwestern Palearctic, it spans from North Africa to western Siberia during breeding season, and migrates to northern sub-Saharan Africa for winter.

Named by the renowned Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in 1758, this species was originally categorized under Columba before being moved to Streptopelia in 1855. The name "Turtur," meaning a turtle dove in Latin, originates from its distinctive cooing sound, not the reptile.

The European Turtle Dove is smaller and more slender than many other doves, measuring around 26-28 cm in length, with a wingspan of 47-53 cm. It weighs between 100-156 grams and can be recognized by its brown coloring, black-and-white striped neck patch, and distinctive wedge-shaped tail with a dark center and white borders and tips.

Support this charming bird by providing food and water sources in your garden or local park. By doing so, you'll not only enrich the beauty of nature around you but also contribute to preserving this threatened species for future generations to enjoy!

Discover the captivating Oriental turtle dove, a charming member of the Columbidae family! This bird can be found across Central Asia and as far east as Japan, with several subspecies showcasing intricate plumage patterns. Two main subspecies, the northern ones, stand out: the western Oriental turtle dove (S. o. meena) and the eastern Oriental turtle dove (S. o. orientalis).

The former resides in eastern Asia and Central Asia, while the latter inhabits central Siberia to Japan. They migrate south during winter, with some venturing as far south as Sri Lanka and India. These fascinating birds are known for their ground-foraging habits, making them a beloved sight in many landscapes.

Despite their striking similarity to the Turtle dove, Oriental turtle doves can be larger, sometimes approaching the size of collared doves. Their diverse appearances and captivating migration patterns make them an enchanting addition to any nature lover's appreciation!

Meet the enchanting Pink Pigeon, a captivating avian species exclusive to Mauritius! With its delicate pinkish-grey feathers adorning their head, shoulders, and belly, and a charming dark brown tail with rust hues, this bird is a sight to behold. Their unique appearance is accentuated by a dainty dark pink beak tipped in white, and eyes framed by a ring of vibrant red skin.

This extraordinary creature almost vanished from our world in the 1970s and 1990s, but thanks to the dedicated efforts of the Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust since 1977, their numbers have been gradually increasing. Despite still being classified as Endangered or Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List, every effort is made to protect and support this fascinating bird, which remains one of the rarest pigeons in existence today.

Despite their rare status, you can find these beautiful birds roaming freely on five protected islands within Mauritius, making them a precious treasure to cherish and admire. So let's do our part to ensure that the Pink Pigeon continues to delight visitors for generations to come!

Meet our feathery friend, the Adamawa Turtle Dove! This captivating bird is often found gracing the landscapes of Cameroon, Nigeria, Chad, and even reaching as far west as Gambia, Senegal, and Mali. Known for its striking appearance, this dove boasts a silver-grey face, blue-grey head, and a unique black patch on the shoulder, complemented by a pink belly and breast, and brown upperparts edged with grey-brown.

This fascinating creature can be seen flitting about in various habitats - from open fields to dense forests, even venturing into suburban gardens and farmland. Despite some uncertainties surrounding its migratory habits, one thing is certain - the Adamawa Turtle Dove is a delightful sight to behold! Its social nature often leads it to form flocks of up to 60 birds during the non-breeding season, sometimes even mingling with other dove species.

These charming creatures play an essential role in maintaining balance within their ecosystems by helping control populations of various seeds. It's crucial we protect and support these lovely birds to ensure their continued existence for generations to come!

Greetings! Let's talk about the fascinating Dusky Turtle Dove, a captivating bird from the Columbidae family, found in East Africa and South Arabia. Standing at approximately 28-30 centimeters tall, this dove is predominantly dark gray with striking black patches on its neck and rufous edges on its wings. Its unique features include an orange or yellow eye surrounded by a purple ring, a purplish slate-gray bill, and red legs.

This charming bird prefers the tranquil beauty of the Afrotropical Highlands Biome, residing in forests, forest edges, and woodland gardens at elevations between 1,800 to 3,200 meters. A curious fact is that it feeds on a diverse diet, including seeds, insects, and even mollusks, foraging from the ground during the day, either alone or in small groups of up to 10.

Supporting these delightful creatures can help maintain the health of their natural habitats and contribute to the rich biodiversity of our planet. So, let's do our part and appreciate the Dusky Turtle Dove for its stunning appearance and fascinating behavior!

Discover the captivating Eurasian collared dove, a delightful avian species that graces continents like Europe, Asia, and Africa, as well as exotic locales such as North America and the Caribbean islands!

This charming dove is named after its distinctive collar—a narrow, contrasting band of dark feathers encircling its neck. The name Streptopelia decaocto comes from the Ancient Greek words for 'collar' (streptos) and 'dove' (peleia), while the number eighteen (decaocto) in its scientific epithet, holds a fascinating story linked to an old Greek myth.

Weighing around 125 grams with an average length of 32 cm and a wingspan of 47 cm, the Eurasian collared dove is medium-sized and slimmer than the wood pigeon, but larger than its cousin, the European turtle dove. Its plumage varies from greyish-buff to pinkish-grey, with a blue-gray underwing patch that adds an extra touch of elegance.

Their gentle cooing is a soothing sound in many parts of the world, making them beloved by humans and deserving of our care and support. By nurturing habitats for these enchanting creatures, we can contribute to a harmonious coexistence between nature and humanity!

Discover the captivating African Collared Dove (Streptopelia roseogrisea)! This delightful little bird can be found in the arid lands of the Sahel, Northern Africa, and even southwestern Arabia, always near water sources.

Reaching lengths of about 26 cm and weighing between 130-166 grams, these birds boast a unique appearance with a greyish brown upper body and pinkish head that gradually fades into a white belly. The wings have a striking bluish tinge! Did you know that this species has no sexual dimorphism?

The African Collared Dove is the wild ancestor of the domestic Barbary dove, sharing similarities with some theories suggesting connections to the Eurasian Collared Dove as well. These birds enjoy a varied diet of grass seeds, plants, berries, insects, and even snails!

So why not lend them your support? Their vibrant colors and friendly nature make these small wonders worth cherishing in our world!

locations: native from Europe to China; widely introduced elsewhere

data.summary:
Meet the enchanting Sunda Collared Dove, a captivating avian resident of Indonesia's lush tropical islands! This medium-sized bird is a delightful sight with its rosy neck and chest, gradually fading into a warm caramel underside. A unique feature that sets it apart from other doves is the dusty black band across its neck, adorned by a white highlight above.

The Sunda Collared Dove's diet is versatile, feeding on grains, seeds, berries, and even insects when needed. Unlike its Eurasian cousin, this dove prefers the tranquil beauty of Indonesia's forests over bustling urban areas, making it a less common but equally cherished sight in nature.

While the Eurasian Collared Dove has gained global fame due to its adaptability and expansion across continents, the Sunda Collared Dove remains relatively unknown, confined to the enchanting islands of Indonesia. Supporting this fascinating creature means preserving their vibrant habitats and allowing them to continue gracing our world with their unique beauty.

Discover the captivating Philippine collared dove! This enchanting bird resides in the beautiful islands of the Philippines, known locally as 'bato-bato de collar'. Its appearance is striking with a brownish body, a gray head, and a unique pinkish neck and breast. Interestingly, it was introduced to Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands, yet its population on Guam has been significantly impacted by invasive brown tree snakes.

This species favors open forests and city edges, demonstrating adaptability in both natural and urban environments. Its melodious call echoes a somewhat poignant 'cOO-cuh-COO', adding to the allure of this fascinating creature. Though it shares some similarities with the Sunda collared dove, the Philippine collared dove can be distinguished by its stronger black and white tail contrast and paler collar with a green sheen.

Sadly, this charming bird faces declining numbers due to intense competition from Spotted doves and Red collared doves in its native range. To help preserve its future, conservation efforts are underway to better understand the threats it faces and determine appropriate actions for its protection. Let's show our support for this remarkable dove by learning more about its unique characteristics and advocating for its conservation!

Discover the captivating Burmese collared dove! This remarkable bird is a vibrant member of the Columbidae family, distinguished by its radiant yellow eye ring and slightly darker plumage compared to its Eurasian cousin. Much like a melody with two notes, its soothing coo can be heard in contrast to the three-note tune of the Eurasian collared dove.

Native to the heartland of Myanmar, nestled within the mighty Irrawaddy River basin, this charming creature calls this beautiful region home. Though it's also reported near neighboring China, further investigation is needed for confirmation.

By supporting these lovely birds, we help ensure their unique song continues to fill the skies of their native lands. So why not lend a helping wing to these enchanting feathered friends?

Meet our feathered friend, the Red-eyed Dove (Streptopelia semitorquata)! This captivating dove is found throughout Sub-Saharan Africa, making it a common sight across much of this region.

The Red-eyed Dove stands out with its striking features – sporting a pale bluish gray forehead that transitions into a darker crown, and a wine-red breast and underparts. Its upperparts are a grayish brown, while the outer wing-coverts are dark slaty blue. The species is unique, with no subspecies recognized.

One of its most intriguing aspects is the vivid coloration of its eyes – ranging from yellowish to orange to red or even orange-brown! Its orbital skin is a striking purplish red, and the bill and legs are black. This stunning dove averages 30 cm in length and weighs between 162–310 grams, making it one of the larger "ringneck" species of doves.

You might spot this majestic bird in various habitats, except for desert zones – they're not partial to sand dunes! In fact, they prefer living near rivers and forests. Despite their widespread presence, Red-eyed Doves are not overly social birds, often feeding alone or in pairs. They build a nest high up in trees and lay two white eggs.

So, the next time you visit sub-Saharan Africa, keep an eye out for this charming bird! Its vibrant eyes and distinctive appearance make it a true gem among the avian world. Let's cherish and support these beautiful creatures that call our diverse continent home.

Discover the captivating Mourning Collared Dove, a fascinating bird found across Africa south of the Sahara! This medium-sized pigeon is easily recognizable by its black plumage with pale brown wings, and a distinctive black patch on the neck edged in white. When they fly, you'll notice their quick, rhythmic wing beats, punctuated by sharp flicks – truly a sight to behold!

These sociable doves prefer habitats near water and often gather together for feeding. Their call is a unique deep, nasally hoot that echoes through the air. Despite the name, they are not closely related to North American Mourning Doves but share similar characteristics with the Red-eyed dove.

Supporting these charming birds can help create a vibrant and diverse ecosystem for future generations to enjoy. Whether you're an avid bird watcher or simply appreciate their beauty, take a moment to appreciate the Mourning Collared Dove.

Meet the Vinaceous Dove! This small, friendly pigeon is a common sight in central and western Africa, particularly in countries like Senegal, Gambia, Burkina Faso, Benin, and Nigeria. With its stocky build and distinctive pinkish-grey underparts contrasting with a pale brown back, wings, and tail, it's easy to spot these birds among the savannah.

The Vinaceous Dove is known for its gregarious nature, often seen feeding in large groups alongside other dove species. They are not picky eaters, consuming seeds, fruits, and even small insects when they can find them. Despite their humble diet, these birds bring a touch of charm to the African landscapes with their quick, characteristic flights, marked by occasional sharp wing flicks.

Supporting these delightful creatures could mean contributing to the rich biodiversity of Africa's savannah ecosystems. So why not take a moment to appreciate the Vinaceous Dove the next time you visit this beautiful continent?

Meet the Ring-necked Dove, a captivating bird species residing in East and southern Africa! Known also as the Cape turtle dove or half-collared dove, this creature is recognized by its distinctive black semi-collar on the lower nape.

Their plumage varies from light to almost sooty, with a unique tail pattern showcased during flight. Males are slightly larger than females and share the same appearance. They're most noticeable in diverse habitats like savannah, farmlands, woodland types, and open plantations.

Despite their adaptability, these doves face threats from predators such as falcons, hawks, wildcats, and introduced grey squirrels. However, they form large flocks at waterholes in dry regions, creating a comforting symphony with their rhythmic cooing sounds that echo across the landscape year-round.

Support these charming creatures by providing them with access to surface water, particularly in arid environments where resources are scarce. By doing so, you'll help ensure that the melodious ring-necked dove continues to charm us with its presence for generations to come!

In the heart of Africa's vibrant landscapes dwell enchanting white-winged collared doves! These graceful birds are part of the Columbidae family, easily recognizable by their stunning white wing patches against their soft grayish-brown bodies. They are true urbanites, flourishing not just in lush forests but also in plantations and bustling cities. These peaceful creatures, native to Ethiopia, Kenya, and Somalia, have a special charm that captivates all who cross paths with them. Their unique beauty makes them more than just ordinary birds - they are the elegant jewels of African skies! Supporting these delightful doves helps preserve this precious piece of nature in our ever-expanding urban environments.

Discover the enchanting Spotted Dove, a captivating small pigeon with a long tail, native to the Indian subcontinent and East Asia! This versatile bird has become a resident in many parts of the world due to introductions, often flourishing in urban areas just as much as forests.

Their unique appearance features a buff-brown body adorned with a distinctive black collar patch that sports white spots on the back and neck sides. A charming feature is their tail tips' contrasting white hue, while their wing coverts boast light buff spots. This diversity in appearance extends across their extensive range, earning them various nicknames like "mountain dove" or "pearl-necked dove."

Despite their beauty, these friendly birds might need our support, as they face challenges adapting to new environments following introductions. So let's cherish and protect this fascinating creature!

Discover the delightful Laughing Dove (Spilopelia senegalensis), a petite pigeon species that calls Africa, the Middle East, South Asia, and Western Australia its home! This charming dove is recognized by its long tail and can often be seen in pairs, feeding peacefully on the ground within dry scrub and semi-desert habitats.
